_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for New Hanover County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find in the state's premier port city.
display_name: New Hanover County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Wilmington, NC — 316 Princess Street (replaced by modern judicial building; historic structure preserved)

previous_courthouses.fate: Multiple early structures served as courthouse and jail. Records were moved between buildings as the town grew.
Pre-Civil War courthouse. Survived the war and Federal occupation. Replaced by the 1892 building when the county outgrew it.

record_losses.description: A fire in the late 18th century damaged some early court records. The bound deed books and most will books survived because they were stored separately. The loss is significant for colonial-era research but does not create a catastrophic gap — New Hanover County's records are generally better preserved than most eastern NC counties because Wilmington's status as the leading city in the colony meant greater institutional care.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Despite this early fire, New Hanover County has among the best colonial-era record survival in eastern NC. Gaps in {ancestor_name}'s record trail here before 1800 may reflect the fire, but the deed chain is largely intact.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.

records_destroyed: Alex Manly's Daily Record press and files
personal papers and business records of expelled Black citizens
records_survived: city government records (though the government was illegitimate after November 10)
record_losses.description: The 1898 coup did not destroy courthouse records — the violence targeted Black institutions, not the county courthouse. However, the destruction of the Daily Record and the forced expulsion of over 2,000 Black citizens resulted in the loss of irreplaceable community records: church records carried by fleeing congregants, business ledgers, personal papers, and the newspaper's files. The coup regime then rewrote official records to legitimize the overthrow, making post-1898 government records unreliable as historical evidence for what actually happened.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1898 coup created a deliberate record gap for Black families in Wilmington. If {ancestor_name} was a Black resident of Wilmington who disappeared from records between 1898 and 1910, the coup — not voluntary migration — is the most likely explanation. The records that survive were created by the regime that committed the violence.
record_losses.source: Umfleet, LeRae. A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot. Raleigh: NC Office of Archives and History, 2009.

deeds.completeness: Good — among the earliest and most complete deed records in NC, reflecting Wilmington's status as a colonial port city
deeds.gaps: Some colonial-era volumes show water damage. No catastrophic gaps.
deeds.repository: New Hanover County Register of Deeds, Wilmington; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from county formation. Wilmington's merchant class generated extensive estate documentation.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the colonial period are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: New Hanover County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from the colonial period. Registers more complete than most eastern NC counties due to Wilmington's urban record-keeping.
marriages.gaps: Some gaps in the 1740s-1760s. Post-1865 Black marriage records begin with Freedmen's Bureau registrations.
marriages.repository: New Hanover County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court of Pleas and Quarter Sessions minutes nearly continuous from 1738. Superior Court records begin 1807.
court_minutes.gaps: Some damage from the 1798 fire to early volumes. Colonial-era minutes are thin for some years.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; New Hanover County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Wilmington city records may predate county registration. Some Wilmington vital records exist from the 1860s-1870s. Statewide compliance improved after 1920.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates; Wilmington City Archives

substitute_sources.description: Wilmington had continuous newspaper publication from the colonial period. The Wilmington Journal (1844-1861), Wilmington Morning Star (1867-present), and Wilmington Messenger carried obituaries, marriage notices, court proceedings, shipping intelligence, and social columns. The Daily Record (Alex Manly's Black newspaper, destroyed 1898) was the nation's only Black daily — its loss is irreplaceable.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives microfilm; Newspapers.com digitization (Morning Star); New Hanover County Public Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Wilmington families. Newspapers name individuals in social columns, obituaries, court reports, and shipping news. For the pre-1913 era, newspaper death notices may be the only record of a death.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Wilmington's status as NC's primary port generated extensive shipping records: customs house manifests, ship registrations, cargo lists, and crew rosters. These records document the maritime workforce — sailors, stevedores, pilots, and merchants — who would not appear in standard land-based records.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (RG 36 — Customs Service); NC State Archives; Cape Fear Museum of History and Science
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for ancestors in the maritime economy. Customs manifests name ship captains and sometimes crew. Cargo lists document the commercial connections that drove Wilmington's economy.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Customs House Records, Port of Wilmington.

substitute_sources.description: St. James Church, the oldest church in Wilmington (founded 1729), maintained parish registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials from the colonial period. The records document the city's Anglican/Episcopal elite and include some records of enslaved people baptized by the congregation.
substitute_sources.repository: St. James Episcopal Church, Wilmington; Diocese of East Carolina archives; transcriptions published by the NC Genealogical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Excellent for colonial and antebellum elite families. Parish registers include entries for enslaved people that may be the only record of their existence.
substitute_sources.source: St. James Episcopal Church Records, transcribed in NC Genealogical Society Journal, various issues.

narrative_hooks: New Hanover County's courthouse survived the Civil War, the 1898 coup, and 250 years of hurricanes — if {ancestor_name} bought or sold property in Wilmington, the deed is almost certainly still in the vault, with a chain of title running back to the colonial grants.

The 1898 coup did not burn the courthouse, but it destroyed something harder to replace — the Daily Record's files, the business records of expelled Black citizens, and the institutional memory of Wilmington's Black community. If {ancestor_name} was part of that community, the records that survive were written by the people who destroyed it.

Wilmington's newspapers are among the most complete in North Carolina — the Morning Star alone runs continuously from 1867, and for {ancestor_name}, a death notice or social column mention may fill gaps that no courthouse record can.

If {ancestor_name} worked on the Wilmington waterfront, the customs house records at the National Archives may hold shipping manifests, crew lists, and cargo records that document a working life invisible in the county courthouse.

St. James Church's registers go back to 1729 — if {ancestor_name}'s family was Anglican or Episcopal in colonial Wilmington, the baptism, marriage, and burial records at St. James may be the oldest surviving documentation of the family in North Carolina.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Umfleet, LeRae. A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot. Raleigh: NC Office of Archives and History, 2009.
Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2003.
Lee, Lawrence. The Lower Cape Fear in Colonial Days. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1965.
Wrenn, Tony P. Wilmington, North Carolina: An Architectural and Historical Portrait. Wilmington: Junior League of Wilmington, 1984.
